content:
# Luke Chapter 22 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 115-117 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_022_page_001.jpg, LUK_022_page_002.jpg, LUK_022_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Now the feast of Unleavened Bread, which is called the Passover, was drawing near. **2** And the chief priests and the scribes were seeking how they might destroy him, for they feared the people.

**3** And Satan entered into Judas, the one called Iscariot, who was of the number of the twelve. **4** And having gone away, he conferred with the chief priests and officers how he might hand him over to them. **5** And they rejoiced, and agreed to give him money. **6** And he consented, and was seeking an opportunity to hand him over to them apart from the crowd.

**7** And the day of Unleavened Bread came, on which it was necessary for the Passover lamb to be sacrificed. **8** And he sent Peter and John, saying, "Go and prepare for us the Passover, that we may eat it."

**9** And they said to him, "Where do you want us to prepare?"

**10** And he said to them, "Behold, when you have entered the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet you; follow him into the house into which he enters. **11** And you shall say to the master of the house, 'The Teacher says to you, Where is the guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?' **12** And he will show you a large upper room, furnished; there make ready." **13** And having gone, they found it just as he had told them, and they prepared the Passover.

**14** And when the hour came, he reclined at table, and the apostles with him. **15** And he said to them, "With desire I have desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer; **16** for I say to you that I shall not eat it until it is fulfilled in the kingdom of God."

**17** And having received a cup, having given thanks, he said, "Take this and divide it among yourselves; **18** for I say to you that I shall not drink of the fruit of the vine from now on until the kingdom of God comes."

**19** And having taken bread, having given thanks, he broke it and gave it to them, saying, "This is my body, which is given for you; do this in my remembrance." **20** And the cup likewise after they had eaten, saying, "This cup is the new covenant in my blood, which is poured out for you.

**21** "But behold, the hand of the one who betrays me is with me on the table. **22** For the Son of Man indeed goes according to what has been determined, but woe to that man by whom he is betrayed." **23** And they began to inquire among themselves which of them it might be who was about to do this.

**24** And a dispute also arose among them as to which of them should be considered the greatest. **25** And he said to them, "The kings of the nations lord it over them, and those who exercise authority over them are called benefactors. **26** But you shall not be so; rather, let the greatest among you become as the youngest, and the one who leads as the one who serves. **27** For who is greater — the one who reclines at table, or the one who serves? Is it not the one who reclines? Yet I am in your midst as the one who serves.

**28** "You are the ones who have continued with me in my trials; **29** and I appoint to you, just as my Father appointed to me, a kingdom, **30** that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom, and you shall sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel.

**31** "Simon, Simon, behold, Satan has demanded to sift you all like wheat; **32** but I have prayed for you, that your faith may not fail; and you, when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers."

**33** And he said to him, "Lord, with you I am ready to go both to prison and to death."

**34** And he said, "I tell you, Peter, the rooster shall not crow today until you have three times denied that you know me."

**35** And he said to them, "When I sent you out without purse and bag and sandals, did you lack anything?" And they said, "Nothing."

**36** And he said to them, "But now, let the one who has a purse take it, and likewise a bag; and the one who has no sword, let him sell his garment and buy one. **37** For I say to you that this which is written must be fulfilled in me: 'And he was reckoned among the lawless.' For indeed, what concerns me has its fulfillment."

**38** And they said, "Lord, behold, here are two swords." And he said to them, "It is enough."

**39** And having gone out, he went according to his custom to the Mount of Olives; and the disciples also followed him. **40** And having come to the place, he said to them, "Pray that you may not enter into temptation."

**41** And he withdrew from them about a stone's throw, and having knelt down, he was praying, **42** saying, "Father, if you are willing, take this cup away from me; yet not my will, but yours be done."

**43** [And an angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him. **44** And being in agony, he was praying more earnestly; and his sweat became like drops of blood falling down upon the ground.]

**45** And having risen from prayer, having come to the disciples, he found them sleeping from grief, **46** and he said to them, "Why do you sleep? Rise and pray, that you may not enter into temptation."

**47** While he was still speaking, behold, a crowd, and the one called Judas, one of the twelve, was going before them; and he drew near to Jesus to kiss him. **48** But Jesus said to him, "Judas, do you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?"

**49** And those around him, seeing what was about to happen, said, "Lord, shall we strike with the sword?" **50** And a certain one of them struck the servant of the high priest and cut off his right ear.

**51** But Jesus answered and said, "Permit even this." And having touched the ear, he healed him.

**52** And Jesus said to those who had come out against him — the chief priests and officers of the temple and elders — "As against a robber have you come out with swords and clubs? **53** When I was with you daily in the temple, you did not stretch out your hands against me. But this is your hour, and the authority of darkness."

**54** And having seized him, they led him away and brought him into the house of the high priest. And Peter was following at a distance. **55** And when they had kindled a fire in the midst of the courtyard and had sat down together, Peter was sitting among them. **56** And a certain servant girl, seeing him sitting by the light, and having gazed at him, said, "This one also was with him."

**57** But he denied it, saying, "Woman, I do not know him."

**58** And after a little while, another saw him and said, "You also are one of them." But Peter said, "Man, I am not."

**59** And after about one hour had passed, a certain other man was insisting strongly, saying, "In truth this one also was with him, for he is also a Galilean."

**60** But Peter said, "Man, I do not know what you are saying." And immediately, while he was still speaking, the rooster crowed. **61** And the Lord, having turned, looked at Peter. And Peter remembered the word of the Lord, how he had said to him, "Before the rooster crows today, you shall deny me three times." **62** And having gone outside, he wept bitterly.

**63** And the men who were holding him were mocking him, beating him; **64** and having blindfolded him, they were demanding of him, saying, "Prophesy! Who is the one who struck you?" **65** And many other things they were saying against him, blaspheming.

**66** And when it became day, the council of the elders of the people was gathered together, both chief priests and scribes, and they led him away into their council, saying, **67** "If you are the Christ, tell us." And he said to them, "If I tell you, you will by no means believe; **68** and if I ask you, you will by no means answer. **69** But from now on, the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God."

**70** And they all said, "You then are the Son of God?" And he said to them, "You yourselves say that I am."

**71** And they said, "What further need of testimony do we have? For we ourselves have heard from his own mouth."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

I have examined the three manuscript pages (LUK_022_page_001.jpg through _003.jpg) showing Codex Sinaiticus folios covering Luke 22. Page 1 spans approximately Lk 21:24 to 22:11; page 2 spans approximately 22:20 to 22:44; page 3 spans approximately 22:52 to 23:7. The text is in continuous scriptio continua, four columns per page, in Greek biblical uncial with standard nomina sacra.

### Critical Variant: Verses 43-44 (The Agony and Bloody Sweat)

This is the most significant textual variant in Luke 22 and one of the most debated in the entire New Testament. On manuscript page 2, column 4 (rightmost column), the text of verses 43-44 is **present in the original hand** of Codex Sinaiticus. All three translators confirmed this reading:

- **EMET** included them in brackets, noting the UBS committee's {C} rating and that some early witnesses (P75, Vaticanus, Alexandrinus) omit them.
- **LOGOS** included them without brackets, noting their absence from P75 and Vaticanus.
- **PNEUMA** included them without brackets, providing extensive discussion of the variant.

**SOPHIA's decision:** I retain verses 43-44 with brackets, following EMET's practice. The brackets signal to the reader that while these verses are present in Sinaiticus (our governing manuscript), their absence from P75 and Vaticanus means their originality is genuinely uncertain. The verses may represent a very early (pre-second-century) tradition that entered the manuscript stream. Including them respects Sinaiticus; bracketing them respects the full manuscript evidence. This is the most honest approach.

### The Long Text of the Last Supper (vv. 19b-20)

Sinaiticus preserves the "longer text" with the cup-bread-cup sequence (vv. 17, 19-20). All three translators followed Sinaiticus here. The Western text (Codex Bezae and some Old Latin) omits vv. 19b-20. SOPHIA retains the full Sinaiticus reading.

### Translation Principles Applied

The SOPHIA consensus favors formal equivalence as the default register — close to the Greek word order and structure — while accepting dynamic improvements where they genuinely clarify without losing meaning. EMET provides the structural backbone, LOGOS the balanced phrasing, and PNEUMA the communicative clarity.

### Verse-by-Verse Divergence Analysis

**Verse 1:** EMET: "the feast of the unleavened-bread was drawing-near, the [one] being-called Pascha"; LOGOS: "the feast of Unleavened Bread, which is called the Passover, was drawing near"; PNEUMA: "The Festival of Unleavened Bread — called the Passover — was approaching." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's clean rendering. "Passover" is used throughout rather than EMET's transliterated "Pascha," since the English reader is served by the translated term. "Drawing near" is preferred over PNEUMA's "approaching" as closer to the Greek engizein.

**Verse 2:** All three agree in substance. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/EMET's phrasing ("seeking how they might destroy him"). PNEUMA's "looking for a way to do away with" is more dynamic than needed.

**Verse 3:** Minor variation. EMET: "being from the number of the twelve"; LOGOS: "being of the number of the twelve"; PNEUMA: "who was one of the Twelve." SOPHIA uses "who was of the number of the twelve," blending LOGOS's formal phrasing with PNEUMA's relative clause for readability.

**Verse 4:** EMET: "spoke-together with"; LOGOS: "conferred with"; PNEUMA: "discussed with." SOPHIA uses "conferred with" (LOGOS) as the best formal equivalent for synelalesen. EMET uses "hand-over," LOGOS "deliver up," PNEUMA "hand over." SOPHIA uses "hand over" — the most natural English equivalent of paradidomi that preserves the physicality of the act.

**Verse 5:** EMET: "silver" (argyrion); LOGOS/PNEUMA: "money." The Greek argyrion literally means "silver/silver coins" but functionally means "money." SOPHIA uses "money" as all three agree the sense is a financial payment, and "money" avoids the archaism of "silver" without losing accuracy.

**Verse 6:** EMET: "favorable-time"; LOGOS: "opportunity"; PNEUMA: "opportunity." Consensus is clear: "opportunity."

**Verse 7:** EMET: "Pascha to be sacrificed"; LOGOS: "Passover to be sacrificed"; PNEUMA: "Passover lamb had to be sacrificed." SOPHIA adds "lamb" with PNEUMA — the Greek to pascha here refers specifically to the sacrificial animal, and "Passover lamb" makes this explicit as it was in the original context.

**Verse 8:** EMET: "Having gone, prepare for us the Pascha that we may eat it"; LOGOS: "Go and prepare for us the Passover, that we may eat"; PNEUMA: "Go and prepare the Passover meal for us, so we can eat it together." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's balanced phrasing, adding "it" for completeness.

**Verse 9:** EMET: "Where do you will that we should prepare?"; LOGOS: "Where do you will that we prepare?"; PNEUMA: "Where do you want us to prepare it?" SOPHIA uses "Where do you want us to prepare?" — adopting PNEUMA's natural "want" over the archaic "will" while keeping the formal question structure.

**Verse 10:** EMET: "jar of water"; LOGOS: "pitcher of water"; PNEUMA: "water jar." The Greek keramion means an earthenware vessel. SOPHIA uses "jar of water" (EMET) as the most accurate and natural rendering.

**Verse 12:** EMET: "having-been-furnished"; LOGOS: "furnished"; PNEUMA: "already furnished and ready." SOPHIA uses "furnished" — the Greek estromenen simply means "furnished/spread" and does not require the addition "and ready."

**Verse 15:** EMET: "With desire I desired to eat this Pascha"; LOGOS: "With desire I have desired to eat this Passover"; PNEUMA: "I have longed intensely to eat this Passover." This is a key decision. The Greek epithymia epethymesa is a Hebraic cognate accusative construction. EMET and LOGOS preserve the literal form; PNEUMA captures the emotional force dynamically. SOPHIA preserves the Hebraism with LOGOS: "With desire I have desired" — this distinctive construction is a signature of Jesus' speech register and should not be flattened.

**Verse 16:** EMET: "I will certainly not eat of it until it should be fulfilled"; LOGOS: "I shall not eat it until it be fulfilled"; PNEUMA: "I will not eat it again until it finds its fulfillment." SOPHIA uses "I shall not eat it until it is fulfilled" — close to LOGOS, with "is fulfilled" instead of the subjunctive "be fulfilled" for modern readability.

**Verse 20:** EMET: "the [one] being-poured-out on-behalf-of you"; LOGOS: "which is poured out for you"; PNEUMA: "which is poured out for you." LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; SOPHIA follows.

**Verse 21:** EMET: "the hand of the [one] handing-over me"; LOGOS: "the hand of the one who delivers me up"; PNEUMA: "the hand of my betrayer." SOPHIA uses "the hand of the one who betrays me" — combining the participial structure of EMET/LOGOS with the verb "betray" for clarity, since paradidomi in this context specifically means betrayal.

**Verse 22:** EMET: "the Son of Man indeed goes"; LOGOS: "the Son of Man goes"; PNEUMA: "The Son of Man is going to his fate." SOPHIA retains "indeed" with EMET (the Greek men emphasizes), while avoiding PNEUMA's interpretive "to his fate." For "handed-over" / "delivered up" / "betrays," SOPHIA uses "betrayed" in this context where the meaning is unambiguous.

**Verse 24:** EMET: "contention"; LOGOS: "dispute"; PNEUMA: "dispute." Consensus: "dispute."

**Verse 25:** All three agree on "benefactors" (euergetai) — a crucial term to preserve.

**Verse 27:** EMET: "I am in the midst of you"; LOGOS: "I am in the midst of you"; PNEUMA: "here I am among you." SOPHIA uses "I am in your midst" — a slight variation that is both natural and formal.

**Verse 29:** EMET: "I appoint to you, just-as my Father appointed to me, a kingdom"; LOGOS: "I appoint to you, even as my Father appointed to me, a kingdom"; PNEUMA: "just as my Father has granted me a kingdom, I am granting you the same." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS's structure with "appoint" (diatithemai) rather than PNEUMA's "granted," preserving the covenantal overtone of the verb.

**Verse 31:** EMET: "Satan demanded to have you to sift you as the wheat"; LOGOS: "Satan has demanded to sift you as wheat"; PNEUMA: "Satan has demanded to sift all of you like wheat." SOPHIA: "Satan has demanded to sift you all like wheat." The plural "you" (hymas) is made explicit with "you all" following PNEUMA's insight, since English "you" obscures the plural. "Like wheat" follows PNEUMA/LOGOS over EMET's "as the wheat."

**Verse 32:** EMET: "I entreated concerning you"; LOGOS: "I have prayed for you"; PNEUMA: "I have prayed for you." SOPHIA follows the consensus "I have prayed for you." EMET's "entreated" for edeethen is accurate but archaic.

**Verse 34:** EMET: "I say to you"; LOGOS: "I tell you"; PNEUMA: "I tell you." Consensus: "I tell you."

**Verse 37:** EMET: "the [thing] concerning me has [its] end" (telos); LOGOS: "that which concerns me has its fulfillment"; PNEUMA: "what is written about me is reaching its conclusion." The Greek telos echei means "has its end/completion." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "has its fulfillment" — preserving the sense of completion without PNEUMA's present-progressive paraphrase. For "lawless-ones" vs. "lawless" vs. "outlaws": SOPHIA uses "lawless" (LOGOS), which preserves the alpha-privative anomos.

**Verse 42:** EMET: "take-away this cup from me; but not my will, but yours let-it-be-done"; LOGOS: "take this cup away from me; nevertheless, not my will but yours be done"; PNEUMA: "take this cup away from me. Yet not my will, but yours be done." SOPHIA uses "yet" (PNEUMA) over "nevertheless" (LOGOS) and "but" (EMET) — it is the most natural adversative in modern English while remaining dignified.

**Verses 43-44:** EMET brackets; LOGOS and PNEUMA do not. See Critical Variant note above. SOPHIA brackets. On vocabulary: EMET: "an agony"; LOGOS: "agony"; PNEUMA: "anguish." SOPHIA uses "agony" — the Greek word is agonia, and the English cognate preserves it. PNEUMA's "anguish" is interpretive. For the sweat: EMET: "drops of blood falling-down upon the earth"; LOGOS: "drops of blood falling down upon the ground"; PNEUMA: "his sweat fell to the ground like drops of blood." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS's structure — "his sweat became like drops of blood falling down upon the ground" — which preserves the Greek egeneto ("became") that PNEUMA restructured.

**Verse 45:** EMET: "sleeping from the grief"; LOGOS: "sleeping from sorrow"; PNEUMA: "asleep, exhausted by grief." The Greek lupes means "grief/sorrow." SOPHIA uses "grief" (EMET/PNEUMA) over "sorrow" (LOGOS) — "grief" is stronger and fits the Passion context.

**Verse 48:** EMET: "with a kiss you hand-over the Son of Man?"; LOGOS: "do you deliver up the Son of Man with a kiss?"; PNEUMA: "are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss?" SOPHIA uses "do you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?" — the question form of LOGOS with the verb "betray" (PNEUMA), which is the precise meaning of paradidomi here.

**Verse 51:** This is one of the most contested phrases in Luke. The Greek eate heos toutou is notoriously ambiguous. EMET: "Permit [it] even [to] this"; LOGOS: "Permit even this"; PNEUMA: "Stop! No more of this!" EMET and LOGOS preserve the ambiguity of the Greek; PNEUMA resolves it decisively as a command to stop. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "Permit even this" — preserving the deliberate compression of the Greek. Jesus may be telling disciples to stop fighting, or telling the arresting party to allow him this final act of healing, or both. The ambiguity is the meaning.

**Verse 52:** EMET: "As against a robber have you come-out"; LOGOS: "As against a robber have you come out"; PNEUMA: "Am I some kind of rebel, that you come after me." The Greek lestes means "robber/bandit/insurrectionist." SOPHIA retains "robber" with EMET/LOGOS — it preserves the legal category while "rebel" (PNEUMA) is interpretive.

**Verse 53:** EMET: "the authority of the darkness"; LOGOS: "the authority of darkness"; PNEUMA: "the hour when darkness rules." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "the authority of darkness" — translating exousia tou skotous directly. PNEUMA's "the hour when darkness rules" is a dynamic paraphrase.

**Verse 56:** EMET: "servant-girl"; LOGOS: "maidservant"; PNEUMA: "servant girl." SOPHIA uses "servant girl" — the most natural English rendering of paidiske.

**Verse 61:** EMET: "looked-at Peter"; LOGOS: "looked upon Peter"; PNEUMA: "looked straight at Peter." The Greek eneblepsen means a direct, intent gaze. SOPHIA uses "looked at Peter" — simple and accurate. PNEUMA's "straight at" adds emphasis not in the Greek, while LOGOS's "upon" is slightly archaic.

**Verse 64:** EMET: "having covered him"; LOGOS: "having blindfolded him"; PNEUMA: "They blindfolded him." Sinaiticus reads perikalypsantes auton — "having covered/veiled him." LOGOS and PNEUMA render this as "blindfolded," which is interpretive but contextually correct (they then ask "who struck you?", implying he cannot see). EMET follows Sinaiticus literally with "covered." SOPHIA uses "blindfolded" with LOGOS/PNEUMA — the covering was specifically for the purpose of blinding, and "blindfolded" communicates the narrative function accurately. However, note that Sinaiticus does NOT add "his face" (to prosopon autou), which appears in some later manuscripts.

**Verse 65:** EMET: "many other things, blaspheming, they were saying against him"; LOGOS: "many other things they were saying against him, blaspheming"; PNEUMA: "they hurled all kinds of insults at him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's word order, which reads naturally while preserving the participial "blaspheming."

**Verse 66:** EMET: "their Sanhedrin"; LOGOS: "their council"; PNEUMA: "their Sanhedrin." The Greek synedrion auton with the possessive pronoun is notable. SOPHIA uses "their council" — using the English term while preserving the possessive pronoun that signals Luke's outsider perspective for Gentile readers.

**Verse 67:** EMET: "the Christ"; LOGOS: "the Christ"; PNEUMA: "the Messiah." SOPHIA uses "the Christ" — this is the Greek title ho Christos, and since it functions as a title (not a name) in this context, the Greek form is appropriate for a translation from a Greek manuscript. PNEUMA's "Messiah" is the Hebrew/Aramaic equivalent, which would be appropriate in a translation from Hebrew sources.

**Verse 69:** All three agree on substance. EMET: "from the now on"; LOGOS: "from now on"; PNEUMA: "from now on." SOPHIA: "from now on."

**Verse 70:** EMET: "You yourselves say that I am"; LOGOS: "You say that I am"; PNEUMA: "You are the ones saying that I am." SOPHIA uses "You yourselves say that I am" — the emphatic hymeis ("you yourselves") is preserved with EMET, which captures the force of Jesus throwing the declaration back onto his accusers.

**Verse 71:** All three essentially agree. Minor word order variations resolved in favor of LOGOS's phrasing.

---

*Produced by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
*The SOPHIA Bible -- Wisdom Through Consilience*
*Source: Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), 4th century CE*
